IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses an important practical problem—improving abstractive sentence summarization—by introducing a selective gate mechanism that yields measurable ROUGE gains on standard datasets (Gigaword, DUC04, MSR-ATC). From my methodology-focused perspective the contribution is a useful architectural refinement that practitioners can adopt, but it is incremental relative to encoder–decoder + attention paradigms and would need thorough reproducibility (code, multiple runs, ablations) to change prevailing practices. It plausibly opens follow-up work on selective filtering and sentence-level gating, but its impact is likely moderate rather than transformative across NLP. | B(3): This paper addresses an important practical subproblem—abstractive sentence summarization—and proposes a clear, interpretable mechanism (a sentence-conditioned selective gate over encoder states) that outperforms strong baselines on standard datasets (Gigaword, DUC-2004, MSR-ATC). The selective-encoding idea is a useful, principled approach to content filtering that connects to and could complement existing selection/copy and attention mechanisms, so researchers building summarizers or content-planning modules may adopt or extend it. However, its scope is limited to sentence-level summarization and the paper does not demonstrate broader or document-level gains, so the likely impact is moderate rather than transformative.

The selective-encoding idea is a useful, principled approach to content filtering that connects to and could complement existing selection/copy and attention mechanisms, so researchers building summarizers or content-planning modules may adopt or extend it. However, its scope is limited to sentence-level summarization and the paper does not demonstrate broader or document-level gains, so the likely impact is moderate rather than transformative.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized and follows a clear structure (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion), with the main contributions stated early and a thorough related-work section. Notation appears consistent and key components (bidirectional GRU encoder, selective gate, attention-equipped GRU decoder) are defined, and figures/tables/examples (datasets, baselines, ROUGE results) seem effective. A minor clarity issue is the selective gate’s high-dimensionality and its visualization being indirect (first-derivative approximation), and the absence of an explicit code release note slightly reduces reproducibility clarity. Overall the writing quality is good with few ambiguous passages, so the presentation merits a strong but not perfect score. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and follows a clear flow from problem motivation to model (encoder -> selective gate -> attention GRU decoder) and experiments on Gigaword, DUC-2004, and MSR-ATC. Main contributions are stated early and related work coverage and hyperparameter reporting are thorough; notation appears consistent and writing quality is good. Figures/tables and examples (ROUGE results vs baselines like ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s) are informative, though the selective gate representation is noted as hard to visualize which slightly weakens exposition of the core mechanism. Overall clarity is strong with only minor presentation issues around interpretability of the selective gate outputs.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses abstractive sentence summarization—a core NLP task—and proposes a clearly articulated selective-encoding architecture evaluated on standard benchmarks (English Gigaword, DUC-2004, MSR-ATC) using accepted metrics (ROUGE-1/2/L). The experimental setup compares against strong, relevant baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, s2s+att) and reports hyperparameters, matching the methodological and evaluation standards expected at a top NLP/ML venue. From a methodological-reviewer perspective the work is well positioned for the community that cares about sequence-to-sequence modeling and summarization innovations. | B(5): This paper fits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses abstractive sentence summarization, a core NLP problem, and proposes a novel selective encoding mechanism that is relevant to sequence-to-sequence and content-selection research. The methodology (biGRU encoder, selective gate, attention-equipped GRU decoder), use of standard datasets (Gigaword, DUC2004, MSR-ATC), comparisons to strong baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, s2s+att), and evaluation with ROUGE metrics match the community standards. The clear positioning against related work and reported hyperparameters further indicate it targets the right audience and expectations for a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses abstractive sentence summarization—a core NLP task—and proposes a clearly articulated selective-encoding architecture evaluated on standard benchmarks (English Gigaword, DUC-2004, MSR-ATC) using accepted metrics (ROUGE-1/2/L). The experimental setup compares against strong, relevant baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, s2s+att) and reports hyperparameters, matching the methodological and evaluation standards expected at a top NLP/ML venue. From a methodological-reviewer perspective the work is well positioned for the community that cares about sequence-to-sequence modeling and summarization innovations.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses abstractive sentence summarization, a core NLP problem, and proposes a novel selective encoding mechanism that is relevant to sequence-to-sequence and content-selection research. The methodology (biGRU encoder, selective gate, attention-equipped GRU decoder), use of standard datasets (Gigaword, DUC2004, MSR-ATC), comparisons to strong baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, s2s+att), and evaluation with ROUGE metrics match the community standards. The clear positioning against related work and reported hyperparameters further indicate it targets the right audience and expectations for a top conference.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper clearly states its main claim (a selective gate that tailors encoder representations improves abstractive sentence summarization) and presents empirical evidence on Gigaword, DUC2004 and MSR-ATC against several published baselines with reported ROUGE scores and hyperparameters. However, the summary lacks mention of critical reproducibility details (code release, multiple runs/seeds, variance or significance testing) and I see no explicit ablation studies isolating the gate, sentence vector, and attention contributions—so it is unclear which component drives gains. Mathematical components appear standard and correct, but the experimental methodology is incomplete for full technical validation. Overall the results are promising but additional controlled experiments and statistical rigor are needed to fully support the claims. | B(4): The paper's main claim—that a selective gate on top of a BiGRU encoder yields improved sentence representations for abstractive summarization—is clearly stated and supported by experimental comparisons to reasonable baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, s2s+att) on standard datasets (Gigaword, MSR-ATC, DUC04 as test). There are no formal proofs needed and the model equations (selective gate, attention-equipped GRU decoder) appear conceptually consistent; hyperparameters and datasets are reported. Methodologically, however, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, code release, or ablations isolating the gate's contribution beyond ROUGE improvements, which are minor gaps that do not undermine the main technical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (a selective gate that tailors encoder representations improves abstractive sentence summarization) and presents empirical evidence on Gigaword, DUC2004 and MSR-ATC against several published baselines with reported ROUGE scores and hyperparameters. However, the summary lacks mention of critical reproducibility details (code release, multiple runs/seeds, variance or significance testing) and I see no explicit ablation studies isolating the gate, sentence vector, and attention contributions—so it is unclear which component drives gains. Mathematical components appear standard and correct, but the experimental methodology is incomplete for full technical validation. Overall the results are promising but additional controlled experiments and statistical rigor are needed to fully support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claim—that a selective gate on top of a BiGRU encoder yields improved sentence representations for abstractive summarization—is clearly stated and supported by experimental comparisons to reasonable baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, s2s+att) on standard datasets (Gigaword, MSR-ATC, DUC04 as test). There are no formal proofs needed and the model equations (selective gate, attention-equipped GRU decoder) appear conceptually consistent; hyperparameters and datasets are reported. Methodologically, however, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, code release, or ablations isolating the gate's contribution beyond ROUGE improvements, which are minor gaps that do not undermine the main technical claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the core, recent baselines for abstractive sentence summarization (ABS, Chopra et al. CAs2s, Nallapati Feats2s, Luong-NMT, s2s+att) on the standard datasets (Gigaword, DUC-2004, MSR-ATC) using ROUGE, which is appropriate and fair. It cites and discusses relevant prior approaches and frames the selective gate relative to attention-based encoder–decoder models. However, the paper summary does not report ablation studies isolating the gate/sentence-vector contributions, multiple-run variance or statistical tests, nor does it mention code release—so while comparisons are strong, experimental analysis of differences and reproducibility details are somewhat lacking. | B(5): The paper compares against the major recent sentence-level abstractive baselines (Rush et al. 2015 ABS, Chopra et al. 2016 CAs2s, Nallapati et al. 2016 Feats2s) and standard seq2seq+attention/NMT variants on the same datasets (Gigaword, DUC-2004, MSR-ATC) using ROUGE metrics, so the choice of baselines is appropriate and contemporaneous. The evaluation appears fair (same data and metrics) and the authors report hyperparameters and note dataset-size limitations (DUC-2004), which supports transparency. They discuss and visualize how the selective gate differs from attention and acknowledge when prior methods are comparable, and the related-work coverage is thorough, situating their selective-encoding contribution relative to existing attentional seq2seq models. Overall the comparisons are comprehensive and convincingly positioned against relevant competing approaches.

The evaluation appears fair (same data and metrics) and the authors report hyperparameters and note dataset-size limitations (DUC-2004), which supports transparency. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates SEASS on standard splits (Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR-ATC) and compares to strong baselines (ABS, ABS+, CAs2s, Feats2s, s2s+att), and reports hyperparameters — so empirical claims are reasonably supported. However, the summary does not indicate targeted ablations isolating the selective gate, sentence vector, or attention components, nor does it report multiple runs/variance or statistical significance, which weakens causal attribution. Related work appears thorough (covers Rush et al. 2015, Chopra et al. 2016, Nallapati et al. 2016), and a limitation (visualization difficulty, DUC small) is acknowledged, but code/reproducibility details (seeds, full training recipe, release) are not mentioned. Overall it is adequate but missing key ablations and statistical/reproducibility rigor expected for strong methodological claims. | B(4): The paper evaluates on standard benchmarks (Gigaword training, DUC-04 and MSR-ATC testsets) and compares to strong baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, s2s+att), which provides solid empirical support for the main performance claims. However, the summary indicates no explicit ablation isolating the selective gate from other model components (only a derivative-based visualization is described) so sensitivity of the key design choice is not fully explored. Related work appears thoroughly covered against contemporaneous abstractive seq2seq and attention models, and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and some limitations (e.g., visualization difficulty, small DUC training size) are only partially discussed. Overall the experiments are solid and convincing but miss some deeper analysis (ablation/failure cases) that would make the evidence exhaustive.

However, the summary does not indicate targeted ablations isolating the selective gate, sentence vector, or attention components, nor does it report multiple runs/variance or statistical significance, which weakens causal attribution. Related work appears thorough (covers Rush et al. 2015, Chopra et al. 2016, Nallapati et al. 2016), and a limitation (visualization difficulty, DUC small) is acknowledged, but code/reproducibility details (seeds, full training recipe, release) are not mentioned. Overall it is adequate but missing key ablations and statistical/reproducibility rigor expected for strong methodological claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The selective encoding (a sentence-level gated filter over encoder hidden states) is a modest architectural innovation on top of well-established attentional seq2seq encoders (e.g., Rush et al. 2015; Chopra et al. 2016; Nallapati et al. 2016). The paper clearly positions and differentiates this gating mechanism, but the idea is a relatively straightforward extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. Given the close overlap with prior attentional RNN summarization work and the triviality of other claimed "novel" elements (bidirectional GRU, SGD, embedding sizes), the contribution reads as incremental yet practically useful rather than highly original. | B(3): The paper proposes a selective gate that filters encoder word representations to build a tailored sentence representation for decoding; this architectural twist is a clear incremental extension of attention-based seq2seq summarizers (e.g., Rush et al. 2015; Chopra et al. 2016) rather than a radical new paradigm. The problem (abstractive sentence summarization) is well-studied, and many core components (bidirectional GRU encoder, attention decoder, training with SGD) overlap strongly with prior work, so novelty lies mainly in the specific gating mechanism and its empirical gains. The authors do differentiate their contribution, but the technique reads as a non-trivial yet still incremental refinement that would be of interest to practitioners rather than a surprise to experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The selective encoding (a sentence-level gated filter over encoder hidden states) is a modest architectural innovation on top of well-established attentional seq2seq encoders (e.g., Rush et al. 2015; Chopra et al. 2016; Nallapati et al. 2016). The paper clearly positions and differentiates this gating mechanism, but the idea is a relatively straightforward extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. Given the close overlap with prior attentional RNN summarization work and the triviality of other claimed "novel" elements (bidirectional GRU, SGD, embedding sizes), the contribution reads as incremental yet practically useful rather than highly original.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a selective gate that filters encoder word representations to build a tailored sentence representation for decoding; this architectural twist is a clear incremental extension of attention-based seq2seq summarizers (e.g., Rush et al. 2015; Chopra et al. 2016) rather than a radical new paradigm. The problem (abstractive sentence summarization) is well-studied, and many core components (bidirectional GRU encoder, attention decoder, training with SGD) overlap strongly with prior work, so novelty lies mainly in the specific gating mechanism and its empirical gains. The authors do differentiate their contribution, but the technique reads as a non-trivial yet still incremental refinement that would be of interest to practitioners rather than a surprise to experts.

main_claims: Introduce a selective encoding model that constructs a tailored sentence representation for abstractive sentence summarization.
The selective gate network filters encoder word representations using a sentence-level vector to provide a second-level representation (h') for the decoder.
The proposed SEASS model (encoder + selective gate + attention-equipped GRU decoder) outperforms state-of-the-art baseline models on standard sentence summarization test sets.
_summary.methodology: A bidirectional GRU encoder produces word-level hidden states; a selective gate network computes a gate per word from the word hidden state and a sentence representation to produce filtered word representations h'; an attention-equipped GRU decoder generates summaries from the tailored representations.
limitations: The selective gate output is high-dimensional and hard to visualize directly (requires approximation via first derivatives).
DUC 2004 is too small to train the neural model (used only as a test set).

reviewer_a.name: Methodology Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with extensive experience implementing and evaluating sequence-to-sequence models for NLP tasks; has led reproducibility efforts and benchmark suites for summarization and translation.
traits: insists on strong, well-tuned baselines and compares against exact implementation variants rather than off-the-shelf numbers
values thorough ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each architectural component (e.g., gate, sentence vector, attention)
looks for clear training details (hyperparameters, data splits, early stopping, random seeds) and reproducible code/data release
checks statistical rigor: multiple runs, variance reporting, and significance testing when claiming improvements
examines implementation/compute cost and whether gains justify additional complexity
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments are sufficient, reproducible, and support the claimed contributions; requests additional controlled experiments where needed.

reviewer_b.name: Domain Impact Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in text generation and summarization, with a track record of work on abstractive methods and an overview of the evolving literature and application needs.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior selective/attention and content-selection mechanisms in summarization and related tasks
assesses broader significance: practical applicability, limitations (e.g., sentence vs. document), and potential to influence follow-up research
values clear conceptual framing that highlights why the proposed selective encoding is a principled or useful advance
looks for connections to related paradigms (copying, content planning, pointer mechanisms) and whether this work complements or supersedes them
notices clarity of exposition and whether the paper communicates insights usable by both researchers and practitioners
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper on its novelty and potential impact within the summarization literature, and on how convincingly it positions itself relative to related work.
